content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Matt Pocock’s updated Skills system shows how to move from a rough idea to researched, tested and reviewed software — without turning one AI conversation into a chaotic mess.
Most AI coding sessions start with a request like this:
“Build an offline-first order system for my application.”
The agent immediately begins writing code.
It creates a database table, adds a synchronization service, updates the interface and writes a few tests.
Then the problems start.
What should happen when the local and server versions conflict?
Should failed requests retry automatically?
Can users edit an order while it is waiting to sync?
How should pending changes appear in the interface?
The agent has already answered these questions through its code — even though nobody explicitly made those decisions.
This is where AI-assisted coding usually becomes expensive.
The model may be fast at producing code, but speed does not help when it is moving in the wrong direction.
In his recent Skills v1.1 walkthrough, Matt Pocock presents a better approach: stop treating an AI coding agent like a machine that receives one giant prompt. Give it a clear engineering process instead.

The real problem is not code generation
Modern coding agents can produce an impressive amount of code.
But software development involves much more than typing:

• Understanding the actual problem

• Researching unfamiliar technology

• Agreeing on important decisions

• Defining what success looks like

• Dividing large work into manageable pieces

• Testing the behaviour

• Reviewing the final result
When all these activities happen inside one long chat, the boundaries disappear.
The agent researches while implementing, redesigns while testing and makes product decisions while editing files.
That can produce a lot of activity without producing reliable software.
Matt’s Skills repository approaches the problem differently. Each skill gives the agent a focused responsibility and defines what it should hand to the next stage.
For most features, the main flow looks like this:
grill-with-docs
↓
to-spec
↓
to-tickets
↓
implement
↓
code-reviewThe repository documents this as its main engineering chain, with /wayfinder acting as an optional starting point for work that is too large or unclear to enter the normal flow directly.
The important idea is simple:
Planning, research, implementation and review should not be the same task.

/research: Give the reading work to an agent
Sometimes you cannot make a good technical decision because you do not yet know enough.
You may need to understand:

• How an SDK behaves

• Whether an operating system supports a requirement

• What an API guarantees

• How a framework handles a particular lifecycle

• Whether a library is still maintained

• What an official specification actually says
Without a clear research process, an agent may rely on memory, blogs or assumptions.
The /research skill is designed to investigate one question using primary sources such as official documentation, source code, specifications and first-party APIs. It then saves the findings in a cited Markdown document that can be used during planning or implementation.
For example, before designing background synchronization in a React Native application, you might research:
/researchInvestigate the limitations of continuous background work on
modern Android and iOS versions.Focus on official platform documentation and explain which
approaches remain reliable after the app is terminated.The output is not supposed to be another casual chat response.
It becomes a project artifact.
That matters because the next agent session does not need to repeat the investigation. It can read the research document and continue from established facts.
This creates an important separation:

• Research discovers facts

• Humans and planning skills make decisions
An agent can determine what Android documentation says.
It should not silently decide whether your application should accept a particular product limitation.

/wayfinder: Use it when the project is still covered in fog
Not every idea is ready to become a specification.
Consider requests such as:

• “Build a generic mobile client for ERP systems.”

• “Replace our legacy architecture without stopping feature development.”

• “Create an AI design tool that generates production-ready components.”

• “Make the entire application work offline.”
These are not single features.
They contain many connected unknowns, and one decision may reveal several new questions.
This is where /wayfinder fits.
Matt describes it as a planning tool for work that is too large for one agent session and too unclear to turn into a specification immediately. It creates a shared map of investigation and decision tickets, then resolves those tickets one at a time until the path becomes clear.
The skill uses three useful ideas.
The destination
Before creating tickets, you define what reaching the end should look like.
The destination might be:

• A completed architecture specification

• A decision about which platform to use

• A migration plan

• A technical design ready for implementation
The destination prevents the investigation from growing forever.
The frontier
The frontier contains questions that are clear enough to investigate now.
For example:

• Can the current backend expose enough metadata for server-driven forms?

• Which screens must work offline?

• Can the existing authentication system support multiple client applications?
These can become tickets because the question is already precise.
The fog
The fog contains questions that you know will matter, but cannot yet describe clearly.
Perhaps the synchronization design depends on what the backend supports. Until that research is complete, creating detailed sync tickets would be guesswork.
As decisions are made, more of the fog becomes visible and turns into new tickets.
This makes /wayfinder different from a normal project plan.
It does not pretend that the entire route is already known.
It maps only what can currently be seen.
Just as importantly, /wayfinder plans by default—it does not build the final product. Its goal is to remove uncertainty until the work can move into /to-spec or directly into implementation when the remaining change is small.

/to-spec: Turn shared understanding into a stable document
Once the important questions have been answered, you need something more reliable than chat history.
That is the role of /to-spec.
It converts the current conversation and codebase understanding into a structured specification. It is intentionally not another interview: the alignment work should already be complete, so the skill synthesizes what is known instead of restarting the discussion.
The resulting specification includes areas such as:

• The problem being solved

• The high-level solution

• User stories

• Decisions already made

• Testing boundaries

• Out-of-scope items

• Completion criteria
One particularly useful part is the focus on testing seams.
A seam is a stable interface where behaviour can be tested.
For an offline order feature, possible seams might include:
createOfflineOrder(input)
syncPendingOrders()
resolveOrderConflict(local, remote)The implementation behind those functions can change.
The tests remain valuable because they target stable behaviour rather than internal details.
A good specification reduces a dangerous habit in AI coding: repeatedly reopening settled decisions.
Once the spec says that the server version wins during a conflict, the implementation agent should not invent a different strategy halfway through coding.

/to-tickets: Slice the feature into working behaviour
A specification may still be too large for one agent session.
/to-tickets breaks it into smaller tickets, but it does not simply divide the work by technical layer.
A weak ticket breakdown might look like this:
Ticket 1: Create database tables
Ticket 2: Create API methods
Ticket 3: Create Redux state
Ticket 4: Build the interface
Ticket 5: Add testsNone of those tickets produces working behaviour by itself.
The database ticket cannot be demonstrated to a user. The interface ticket may depend on everything else. Testing is postponed until the end.
/to-tickets instead prefers tracer-bullet vertical slices: narrow pieces of behaviour that travel through every required layer and remain independently testable or demonstrable.
A stronger breakdown would be:
Ticket 1:
Allow a user to create an order without internet access
and show it as pending.Ticket 2:
Automatically submit pending orders when connectivity returns.Ticket 3:
Show synchronization failures and allow manual retry.Ticket 4:
Detect server conflicts and apply the agreed resolution rule.Each ticket may touch storage, state, APIs, interface code and tests.
But each one delivers a real capability.
The skill also records blocking relationships. When using a supported issue tracker, those relationships can identify which tickets are ready to be worked on and which are waiting for earlier decisions or implementations.
This makes the workflow useful beyond individual developers.
A team — or multiple agent sessions — can work on independent tickets without losing the overall sequence.

/implement: The thinking has already happened
By the time /implement begins, the agent should know:

• What it is building

• Which ticket it is completing

• Which decisions have been settled

• Where the behaviour should be tested

• What “done” means
The implementation skill is deliberately described as the hands rather than the head.
It builds from the specification or tickets, uses test-driven development at the agreed seams, runs type checking regularly, executes focused tests while working and runs the complete test suite before finishing.
The key rule is that implementation should not quietly redesign the feature.
That does not mean the agent must blindly follow a broken plan.
It means that when a new design problem appears, the workflow should recognize it as a decision rather than hiding it inside a code change.
This creates cleaner handoffs.
Planning decides.
Implementation builds.
Tests provide feedback.
Review judges the result.

/code-review: Ask two different questions
A normal code review often mixes everything into one vague conclusion:
“The code looks good.”
But “good” can mean two completely different things.
Did we build it well?
This is the Standards review.
It checks naming, duplication, complexity, project conventions, module boundaries and common code smells.
Did we build the correct thing?
This is the Spec review.
It compares the implementation with the originating specification or ticket.
The code may be clean but miss an important requirement.
It may satisfy the requirement but introduce unnecessary complexity.
Matt’s /code-review skill runs these two reviews separately and reports their findings independently, rather than combining them into one score where success in one area could hide failure in another.
That separation is one of the strongest ideas in the entire workflow.
A beautifully designed synchronization engine is still a failure if the specification required users to approve synchronization manually.

What the complete process looks like
For a normal feature:
1. /grill-with-docs
Resolve requirements and important decisions.2. /research
Investigate external facts whenever needed.3. /to-spec
Write the agreed understanding as a specification.4. /to-tickets
Divide the specification into vertical slices.5. /implement
Build one ticket using tests and type checking.6. /code-review
Review standards and specification compliance separately.For a large and unclear project:
1. /wayfinder
Map the destination, frontier and remaining fog.2. Resolve research, grilling and prototype tickets.3. /to-spec
Convert the cleared path into a specification.4. /to-tickets
Create buildable implementation slices.5. /implement6. /code-review
